It is usually `True` where an estimator has a\\n53:         `metric` or `affinity` or `kernel` parameter with value\\n54:         'precomputed'. Its primary purpose is to support a\\n55:         :term:`meta-estimator` or a cross validation procedure that\\n56:         extracts a sub-sample of data intended for a pairwise\\n57:         estimator, where the data needs to be indexed on both axes.\\n58:         Specifically, this tag is used by\\n59:         `sklearn.utils.metaestimators._safe_split` to slice rows and\\n60:         columns.\\n61: \\n62:         Note that if setting this tag to ``True`` means the estimator can take only\\n63:         positive values, the `positive_only` tag must reflect it and also be set to\\n64:         ``True``.\\n65:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n66: \\n67:     one_d_array: bool = False\\n68:     two_d_array: bool = True\\n69:     three_d_array: bool = False\\n70:     sparse: bool = False\\n71:     categorical: bool = False\\n72:     string: bool = False\\n73:     dict: bool = False\\n74:     positive_only: bool = False\\n75:     allow_nan: bool = False\\n76:     pairwise: bool = False\\n77: \\n78: \\n79: @dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\\n80: class TargetTags:\\n81:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Tags for the target data.\\n82: \\n83:     Parameters\\n84:     ----------\\n85:     required : bool\\n86:         Whether the estimator requires y to be passed to `fit`,\\n87:         `fit_predict` or `fit_transform` methods. InputTags:\\n16:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Tags for the input data.\\n17: \\n18:     Parameters\\n19:     ----------\\n20:     one_d_array : bool, default=False\\n21:         Whether the input can be a 1D array.\\n22: \\n23:     two_d_array : bool, default=True\\n24:         Whether the input can be a 2D array. Note that most common\\n25:         tests currently run only if this flag is set to ``True``.\\n26: \\n27:     three_d_array : bool, default=False\\n28:         Whether the input can be a 3D array.\\n29: \\n30:     sparse : bool, default=False\\n31:         Whether the input can be a sparse matrix.\\n32: \\n33:     categorical : bool, default=False\\n34:         Whether the input can be categorical.\\n35: \\n36:     string : bool, default=False\\n37:         Whether the input can be an array-like of strings.\\n38: \\n39:     dict : bool, default=False\\n40:         Whether the input can be a dictionary.\\n41: \\n42:     positive_only : bool, default=False\\n43:         Whether the estimator requires positive X.\\n44: \\n45:     allow_nan : bool, default=False\\n46:         Whether the estimator supports data with missing values encoded as `np.nan`.\\n47: \\n48:     pairwise : bool, default=False\\n49:         This boolean attribute indicates whether the data (`X`),\\n50:         :term:`fit` and similar methods consists of pairwise measures\\n51:         over samples rather than a feature representation for each\\n52:         sample.  It is usually `True` where an estimator has a\\n53:         `metric` or `affinity` or `kernel` parameter with value\\n54:         'precomputed'. Its primary purpose is to support a\\n55:         :term:`meta-estimator` or a cross validation procedure that\\n56:         extracts a sub-sample of data intended for a pairwise\\n57:         estimator, where the data needs to be indexed on both axes.\\n58:         Specifically, this tag is used by\\n59:         `sklearn.utils.metaestimators._safe_split` to slice rows and\\n60:         columns.\\n61: \\n62:         Note that if setting this tag to ``True`` means the estimator can take only\\n63:         positive values, the `positive_only` tag must reflect it and also be set to\\n64:         ``True``.\\n65:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n66: \\n67:     one_d_array: bool = False\\n68:     two_d_array: bool = True\\n69:     three_d_array: bool = False\\n70:     sparse: bool = False\\n71:     categorical: bool = False\\n72:     string: bool = False\\n73:     dict: bool = False\\n74:     positive_only: bool = False\\n75:     allow_nan: bool = False\\n76:     pairwise: bool = False\\n77: \\n78: \\n79: @dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\\n80: class TargetTags:\\n81:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Tags for the target data.\\n82: \\n83:     Parameters\\n84:     ----------\\n85:     required : bool\\n86:         Whether the estimator requires y to be passed to `fit`,\\n87:         `fit_predict` or `fit_transform` methods. The tag is ``True``\\n88:         for estimators inheriting from `~sklearn.base.RegressorMixin`\\n89:         and `~sklearn.base.ClassifierMixin`.\\n90: \\n91:     one_d_labels : bool, default=False\\n92:         Whether the input is a 1D labels (y).\\n93: \\n94:     two_d_labels : bool, default=False\\n95:         Whether the input is a 2D labels (y).\\n96: \\n97:     positive_only : bool, default=False\\n98:         Whether the estimator requires a positive y (only applicable\\n99:         for regression).\\n100: \\n101:     multi_output : bool, default=False\\n102:         Whether a regressor supports multi-target outputs or a classifier supports\\n103:         multi-class multi-output.\\n104: \\n105:         See :term:`multi-output` in the glossary.\\n106: \\n107:     single_output : bool, default=True\\n108:         Whether the target can be single-output. This can be ``False`` if the\\n109:         estimator supports only multi-output cases.\\n110:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n111: \\n112:     required: bool\\n113:     one_d_labels: bool = False\\n114:     two_d_labels: bool = False\\n115:     positive_only: bool = False\\n116:     multi_output: bool = False\\n117:     single_output: bool = True\\n118: \\n119: \\n120: @dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\\n121: class TransformerTags:\\n122:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Tags for the transformer.\\n123: \\n124:     Parameters\\n125:     ----------\\n126:     preserves_dtype : list[str], default=[\\\"float64\\\"]\\n127:         Applies only on transformers. It corresponds to the data types\\n128:         which will be preserved such that `X_trans.dtype` is the same\\n129:         as `X.dtype` after calling `transformer.transform(X)`. If this\\n130:         list is empty, then the transformer is not expected to\\n131:         preserve the data type. The first value in the list is\\n132:         considered as the default data type, corresponding to the data\\n133:         type of the output when the input data type is not going to be\\n134:         preserved.\\n135:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n136: \\n137:     preserves_dtype: list[str] = field(default_factory=lambda: [\\\"float64\\\"])\\n138: \\n139: \\n140: @dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\\n141: class ClassifierTags:\\n142:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Tags for the classifier.\\n143: \\n144:     Parameters\\n145:     ----------\\n146:     poor_score : bool, default=False\\n147:         Whether the estimator fails to provide a \\\"reasonable\\\" test-set\\n148:         score, which currently for classification is an accuracy of\\n149:         0.83 on ``make_blobs(n_samples=300, random_state=0)``. The\\n150:         datasets and values are based on current estimators in scikit-learn\\n151:         and might be replaced by something more systematic.\\n152: \\n153:     multi_class : bool, default=True\\n154:         Whether the classifier can handle multi-class\\n155:         classification. Note that all classifiers support binary\\n156:         classification. Therefore this flag indicates whether the\\n157:         classifier is a binary-classifier-only or not.\\n158: \\n159:         See :term:`multi-class` in the glossary.\\n160: \\n161:     multi_label : bool, default=False\\n162:         Whether the classifier supports multi-label output: a data point can\\n163:         be predicted to belong to a variable number of classes.\\n164: \\n165:         See :term:`multi-label` in the glossary.\\n166:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n167: \\n168:     poor_score: bool = False\\n169:     multi_class: bool = True\\n170:     multi_label: bool = False\\n171: \\n172: \\n173: @dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\\n174: class RegressorTags:\\n175:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Tags for the regressor.\\n176: \\n177:     Parameters\\n178:     ----------\\n179:     poor_score : bool, default=False\\n180:         Whether the estimator fails to provide a \\\"reasonable\\\" test-set\\n181:         score, which currently for regression is an R2 of 0.5 on\\n182:         ``make_regression(n_samples=200, n_features=10,\\n183:         n_informative=1, bias=5.0, noise=20, random_state=42)``. Don't use this unless\\n236:         you have a *very good* reason.\\n237: \\n238:     input_tags : :class:`InputTags`\\n239:         The input data(X) tags.\\n240:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n241: \\n242:     estimator_type: str | None\\n243:     target_tags: TargetTags\\n244:     transformer_tags: TransformerTags | None = None\\n245:     classifier_tags: ClassifierTags | None = None\\n246:     regressor_tags: RegressorTags | None = None\\n247:     array_api_support: bool = False\\n248:     no_validation: bool = False\\n249:     non_deterministic: bool = False\\n250:     requires_fit: bool = True\\n251:     _skip_test: bool = False\\n252:     input_tags: InputTags = field(default_factory=InputTags)\\n253: \\n254: \\n255: # TODO(1.8): Remove this function\\n256: def default_tags(estimator) -> Tags:\\n257:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Get the default tags for an estimator.\\n258: \\n259:     This ignores any ``__sklearn_tags__`` method that the estimator may have.\\n260: \\n261:     If the estimator is a classifier or a regressor, ``target_tags.required``\\n262:     will be set to ``True``, otherwise it will be set to ``False``.\\n263: \\n264:     ``transformer_tags`` will be set to :class:`~.sklearn.utils. TransformerTags` if the\\n265:     estimator has a ``transform`` or ``fit_transform`` method, otherwise it will be set\\n266:     to ``None``.\\n267: \\n268:     ``classifier_tags`` will be set to :class:`~.sklearn.utils.ClassifierTags` if the\\n269:     estimator is a classifier, otherwise it will be set to ``None``.\\n270:     a classifier, otherwise it will be set to ``None``.\\n271: \\n272:     ``regressor_tags`` will be set to :class:`~.sklearn.utils.RegressorTags` if the\\n273:     estimator is a regressor, otherwise it will be set to ``None``.\\n274: \\n275:     Parameters\\n276:     ----------\\n277:     estimator : estimator object\\n278:         The estimator for which to get the default tags.\\n279: \\n280:     Returns\\n281:     -------\\n282:     tags : Tags\\n283:         The default tags for the estimator.\\n284:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n285:     est_is_classifier = getattr(estimator, \\\"_estimator_type\\\", None) == \\\"classifier\\\"\\n286:     est_is_regressor = getattr(estimator, \\\"_estimator_type\\\", None) == \\\"regressor\\\"\\n287:     target_required = est_is_classifier or est_is_regressor\\n288: \\n289:     return Tags(\\n290:         estimator_type=getattr(estimator, \\\"_estimator_type\\\", None),\\n291:         target_tags=TargetTags(required=target_required),\\n292:         transformer_tags=(\\n293:             TransformerTags()\\n294:             if hasattr(estimator, \\\"transform\\\") or hasattr(estimator, \\\"fit_transform\\\")\\n295:             else None\\n296:         ),\\n297:         classifier_tags=ClassifierTags() if est_is_classifier else None,\\n298:         regressor_tags=RegressorTags() if est_is_regressor else None,\\n299:     )\\n300: \\n301: \\n302: # TODO(1.7): Remove this function\\n303: def _find_tags_provider(estimator, warn=True):\\n304:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Find the tags provider for an estimator.\\n305: \\n306:     Parameters\\n307:     ----------\\n308:     estimator : estimator object\\n309:         The estimator to find the tags provider for.\\n310: \\n311:     warn : bool, default=True\\n312:         Whether to warn if the tags provider is not found.\\n313: \\n314:     Returns\\n315:     -------\\n316:     tag_provider : str\\n317:         The tags provider for the estimator. Can be one of:\\n318:         - \\\"_get_tags\\\": to use the old tags infrastructure\\n319:         - \\\"__sklearn_tags__\\\": to use the new tags infrastructure\\n320:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n321:     mro_model = type(estimator).mro()\\n322:     tags_mro = OrderedDict()\\n323:     for klass in mro_model:\\n324:         tags_provider = []\\n325:         if \\\"_more_tags\\\" in vars(klass):\\n326:             tags_provider.append(\\\"_more_tags\\\")\\n327:         if \\\"_get_tags\\\" in vars(klass):\\n328:             tags_provider.append(\\\"_get_tags\\\")\\n329:         if \\\"__sklearn_tags__\\\" in vars(klass):\\n330:             tags_provider.append(\\\"__sklearn_tags__\\\")\\n331:         tags_mro[klass.__name__] = tags_provider\\n332: \\n333:     all_providers = set(chain.from_iterable(tags_mro.values()))\\n334:     if \\\"__sklearn_tags__\\\" not in all_providers:\\n335:         # default on the old tags infrastructure\\n336:         return \\\"_get_tags\\\"\\n337: \\n338:     tag_provider = \\\"__sklearn_tags__\\\"\\n339:     for klass in tags_mro:\\n340:         has_get_or_more_tags = any(\\n341:             provider in tags_mro[klass] for provider in (\\\"_get_tags\\\", \\\"_more_tags\\\")\\n342:         )\\n343:         has_sklearn_tags = \\\"__sklearn_tags__\\\" in tags_mro[klass]\\n344: \\n345:         if tags_mro[klass] and tag_provider == \\\"__sklearn_tags__\\\":  # is it empty\\n346:             if has_get_or_more_tags and not has_sklearn_tags:\\n347:                 # Case where a class does not implement __sklearn_tags__ and we fallback\\n348:                 # to _get_tags. It seems that \\\"\\n408:                     \\\"there are no classes that implement `__sklearn_tags__` \\\"\\n409:                     \\\"in the MRO and/or all classes in the MRO call \\\"\\n410:                     \\\"`super().__sklearn_tags__()`. Note that `BaseEstimator` needs to be on the \\\"\\n414:                     \\\"right side of other Mixins in the inheritance order. The \\\"\\n415:                     \\\"default are now used instead since retrieving tags failed. \\\"\\n416:                     \\\"This warning will be replaced by an error in 1.7.\\\",\\n417:                     category=DeprecationWarning,\\n418:                 )\\n419:                 tags = default_tags(estimator)\\n420:             else:\\n421:                 raise\\n422:     else:\\n423:         # from __future__ import annotations\n2: \n3: import warnings\n4: from collections import OrderedDict\n5: from dataclasses import dataclass, field\n6: from itertools import chain\n7: \n8: from .fixes import _dataclass_args\n9: \n10: # Authors: The scikit-learn developers\n11: # SPDX-License-Identifier: BSD-3-Clause\n12: \n13: \n14: @dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\n15: class InputTags:\n16:     \"\"\"Tags for the input data.\n17: \n18:     Parameters\n19:     ----------\n20:     one_d_array : bool, default=False\n21:         Whether the input can be a 1D array.\n22: \n23:     two_d_array : bool, default=True\n24:         Whether the input can be a 2D array. Note that most common\n25:         tests currently run only if this flag is set to ``True``.\n26: \n27:     three_d_array : bool, default=False\n28:         Whether the input can be a 3D array.\n29: \n30:     sparse : bool, default=False\n31:         Whether the input can be a sparse matrix.\n32: \n33:     categorical : bool, default=False\n34:         Whether the input can be categorical.\n35: \n36:     string : bool, default=False\n37:         Whether the input can be an array-like of strings.\n38: \n39:     dict : bool, default=False\n40:         Whether the input can be a dictionary.\n41: \n42:     positive_only : bool, default=False\n43:         Whether the estimator requires positive X.\n44: \n45:     allow_nan : bool, default=False\n46:         Whether the estimator supports data with missing values encoded as `np.nan`.\n47: \n48:     pairwise : bool, default=False\n49:         This boolean attribute indicates whether the data (`X`),\n50:         :term:`fit` and similar methods consists of pairwise measures\n51:         over samples rather than a feature representation for each\n52:         sample.  It is usually `True` where an estimator has a\n53:         `metric` or `affinity` or `kernel` parameter with value\n54:         'precomputed'. Its primary purpose is to support a\n55:         :term:`meta-estimator` or a cross validation procedure that\n56:         extracts a sub-sample of data intended for a pairwise\n57:         estimator, where the data needs to be indexed on both axes.\n58:         Specifically, this tag is used by\n59:         `sklearn.utils.metaestimators._safe_split` to slice rows and\n60:         columns.\n61: \n62:         Note that if setting this tag to ``True`` means the estimator can take only\n63:         positive values, the `positive_only` tag must reflect it and also be set to\n64:         ``True``.\n65:     \"\"\"\n66: \n67:     one_d_array: bool = False\n68:     two_d_array: bool = True\n69:     three_d_array: bool = False\n70:     sparse: bool = False\n71:     categorical: bool = False\n72:     string: bool = False\n73:     dict: bool = False\n74:     positive_only: bool = False\n75:     allow_nan: bool = False\n76:     pairwise: bool = False\n77: \n78: \n79: @dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\n80: class TargetTags:\n81:     \"\"\"Tags for the target data.\n82: \n83:     Parameters\n84:     ----------\n85:     required : bool\n86:         Whether the estimator requires y to be passed to `fit`,\n87:         `fit_predict` or `fit_transform` methods. The tag is ``True``\n88:         for estimators inheriting from `~sklearn.base.RegressorMixin`\n89:         and `~sklearn.base.ClassifierMixin`.\n90: \n91:     one_d_labels : bool, default=False\n92:         Whether the input is a 1D labels (y).\n93: \n94:     two_d_labels : bool, default=False\n95:         Whether the input is a 2D labels (y).\n96: \n97:     positive_only : bool, default=False\n98:         Whether the estimator requires a positive y (only applicable\n99:         for regression).\n100: \n101:     multi_output : bool, default=False\n102:         Whether a regressor supports multi-target outputs or a classifier supports\n103:         multi-class multi-output.\n104: \n105:         See :term:`multi-output` in the glossary.\n106: \n107:     single_output : bool, default=True\n108:         Whether the target can be single-output. This can be ``False`` if the\n109:         estimator supports only multi-output cases.\n110:     \"\"\"\n111: \n112:     required: bool\n113:     one_d_labels: bool = False\n114:     two_d_labels: bool = False\n115:     positive_only: bool = False\n116:     multi_output: bool = False\n117:     single_output: bool = True\n118: \n119: \n120: @dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\n121: class TransformerTags:\n122:     \"\"\"Tags for the transformer.\n123: \n124:     Parameters\n125:     ----------\n126:     preserves_dtype : list[str], default=[\"float64\"]\n127:         Applies only on transformers. It corresponds to the data types\n128:         which will be preserved such that `X_trans.dtype` is the same\n129:         as `X.dtype` after calling `transformer.transform(X)`. If this\n130:         list is empty, then the transformer is not expected to\n131:         preserve the data type. The first value in the list is\n132:         considered as the default data type, corresponding to the data\n133:         type of the output when the input data type is not going to be\n134:         preserved.\n135:     \"\"\"\n136: \n137:     preserves_dtype: list[str] = field(default_factory=lambda: [\"float64\"])\n138: \n139: \n140: @dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\n141: class ClassifierTags:\n142:     \"\"\"Tags for the classifier.\n143: \n144:     Parameters\n145:     ----------\n146:     poor_score : bool, default=False\n147:         Whether the estimator fails to provide a \"reasonable\" test-set\n148:         score, which currently for classification is an accuracy of\n149:         0.83 on ``make_blobs(n_samples=300, random_state=0)``. The\n150:         datasets and values are based on current estimators in scikit-learn\n151:         and might be replaced by something more systematic.\n152: \n153:     multi_class : bool, default=True\n154:         Whether the classifier can handle multi-class\n155:         classification. Note that all classifiers support binary\n156:         classification. Therefore this flag indicates whether the\n157:         classifier is a binary-classifier-only or not.\n158: \n159:         See :term:`multi-class` in the glossary.\n160: \n161:     multi_label : bool, default=False\n162:         Whether the classifier supports multi-label output: a data point can\n163:         be predicted to belong to a variable number of classes.\n164: \n165:         See :term:`multi-label` in the glossary.\n166:     \"\"\"\n167: \n168:     poor_score: bool = False\n169:     multi_class: bool = True\n170:     multi_label: bool = False\n171: \n172: \n173: @dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\n174: class RegressorTags:\n175:     \"\"\"Tags for the regressor.\n176: \n177:     Parameters\n178:     ----------\n179:     poor_score : bool, default=False\n180:         Whether the estimator fails to provide a \"reasonable\" test-set\n181:         score, which currently for regression is an R2 of 0.5 on\n182:         ``make_regression(n_samples=200, n_features=10,\n183:         n_informative=1, bias=5.0, noise=20, random_state=42)``. The\n184:         dataset and values are based on current estimators in scikit-learn\n185:         and might be replaced by something more systematic.\n186:     \"\"\"\n187: \n188:     poor_score: bool = False\n189: \n190: \n191: @dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\n192: class Tags:\n193:     \"\"\"Tags for the estimator.\n194: \n195:     See :ref:`estimator_tags` for more information.\n196: \n197:     Parameters\n198:     ----------\n199:     estimator_type : str or None\n200:         The type of the estimator. This is only meant for\n225:         stateless and dummy transformers!\n226: \n227:     non_deterministic : bool, default=False\n228:         Whether the estimator is not deterministic given a fixed ``random_state``.\n229: \n230:     requires_fit : bool, default=True\n231:         Whether the estimator requires to be fitted before calling one of\n232:         `transform`, `predict`, `predict_proba`, or `decision_function`.\n233: \n234:     _skip_test : bool, default=False\n235:         Whether to skip common tests entirely. Don't use this unless\n236:         you have a *very good* reason.\n237: \n238:     input_tags : :class:`InputTags`\n239:         The input data(X) tags.\n240:     \"\"\"\n241: \n242:     estimator_type: str | None\n243:     target_tags: TargetTags\n244:     transformer_tags: TransformerTags | None = None\n245:     classifier_tags: ClassifierTags | None = None\n246:     regressor_tags: RegressorTags | None = None\n247:     array_api_support: bool = False\n248:     no_validation: bool = False\n249:     non_deterministic: bool = False\n250:     requires_fit: bool = True\n251:     _skip_test: bool = False\n252:     input_tags: InputTags = field(default_factory=InputTags)\n253: \n254: \n255: # TODO(1.8): Remove this function\n256: def default_tags(estimator) -> Tags:\n257:     \"\"\"Get the default tags for an estimator.\n258: \n259:     This ignores any ``__sklearn_tags__`` method that the estimator may have.\n260: \n261:     If the estimator is a classifier or a regressor, ``target_tags.required``\n262:     will be set to ``True``, otherwise it will be set to ``False``.\n263: \n264:     ``transformer_tags`` will be set to :class:`~.sklearn.utils. TransformerTags` if the\n265:     estimator has a ``transform`` or ``fit_transform`` method, otherwise it will be set\n266:     to ``None``.\n267: \n268:     ``classifier_tags`` will be set to :class:`~.sklearn.utils.ClassifierTags` if the\n269:     estimator is a classifier, otherwise it will be set to ``None``.\n270:     a classifier, otherwise it will be set to ``None``.\n271: \n272:     ``regressor_tags`` will be set to :class:`~.sklearn.utils.RegressorTags` if the\n273:     estimator is a regressor, otherwise it will be set to ``None``.\n274: \n275:     Parameters\n276:     ----------\n277:     estimator : estimator object\n278:         The estimator for which to get the default tags.\n279: \n280:     Returns\n281:     -------\n282:     tags : Tags\n283:         The default tags for the estimator.\n284:     \"\"\"\n285:     est_is_classifier = getattr(estimator, \"_estimator_type\", None) == \"classifier\"\n286:     est_is_regressor = getattr(estimator, \"_estimator_type\", None) == \"regressor\"\n287:     target_required = est_is_classifier or est_is_regressor\n288: \n289:     return Tags(\n290:         estimator_type=getattr(estimator, \"_estimator_type\", None),\n291:         target_tags=TargetTags(required=target_required),\n292:         transformer_tags=(\n293:             TransformerTags()\n294:             if hasattr(estimator, \"transform\") or hasattr(estimator, \"fit_transform\")\n295:             else None\n296:         ),\n297:         classifier_tags=ClassifierTags() if est_is_classifier else None,\n298:         regressor_tags=RegressorTags() if est_is_regressor else None,\n299:     )\n300: \n301: \n302: # TODO(1.7): Remove this function\n303: def _find_tags_provider(estimator, warn=True):\n304:     \"\"\"Find the tags provider for an estimator.\n305: \n306:     Parameters\n307:     ----------\n308:     estimator : estimator object\n309:         The estimator to find the tags provider for.\n310: \n311:     warn : bool, default=True\n312:         Whether to warn if the tags provider is not found.\n313: \n314:     Returns\n315:     -------\n316:     tag_provider : str\n317:         The tags provider for the estimator. Can be one of:\n318:         - \"_get_tags\": to use the old tags infrastructure\n319:         - \"__sklearn_tags__\": to use the new tags infrastructure\n320:     \"\"\"\n321:     mro_model = type(estimator).mro()\n322:     tags_mro = OrderedDict()\n323:     for klass in mro_model:\n324:         tags_provider = []\n325:         if \"_more_tags\" in vars(klass):\n326:             tags_provider.append(\"_more_tags\")\n327:         if \"_get_tags\" in vars(klass):\n328:             tags_provider.append(\"_get_tags\")\n329:         if \"__sklearn_tags__\" in vars(klass):\n330:             tags_provider.append(\"__sklearn_tags__\")\n331:         tags_mro[klass.__name__] = tags_provider\n332: \n333:     all_providers = set(chain.from_iterable(tags_mro.values()))\n334:     if \"__sklearn_tags__\" not in all_providers:\n335:         # default on the old tags infrastructure\n336:         return \"_get_tags\"\n337: \n338:     tag_provider = \"__sklearn_tags__\"\n339:     for klass in tags_mro:\n340:         has_get_or_more_tags = any(\n341:             provider in tags_mro[klass] for provider in (\"_get_tags\", \"_more_tags\")\n342:         )\n343:         has_sklearn_tags = \"__sklearn_tags__\" in tags_mro[klass]\n344: \n345:         if tags_mro[klass] and tag_provider == \"__sklearn_tags__\":  # is it empty\n346:             if has_get_or_more_tags and not has_sklearn_tags:\n347:                 # Case where a class does not implement __sklearn_tags__ and we fallback\n348:                 # to _get_tags. We should therefore warn for implementing\n349:                 # __sklearn_tags__.\n350:                 tag_provider = \"_get_tags\"\n351:                 break\n352: \n353:     if warn and tag_provider == \"_get_tags\":\n354:         warnings.warn(\n355:             f\"The {estimator.__class__.__name__} or classes from which it inherits \"\n356:             \"use `_get_tags` and `_more_tags`. Please define the \"\n357:             \"`__sklearn_tags__` method, or inherit from `sklearn.base.BaseEstimator` \"\n358:             \"and/or other appropriate mixins such as `sklearn.base.TransformerMixin`, \"\n359:             \"`sklearn.base.ClassifierMixin`, `sklearn.base.RegressorMixin`, and \"\n360:             \"`sklearn.base.OutlierMixin`. From scikit-learn 1.7, not defining \"\n361:             \"`__sklearn_tags__` will raise an error.\",\n362:             category=DeprecationWarning,\n363:         )\n364:     return tag_provider\n365: \n366: \n367: def get_tags(estimator) -> Tags:\n368:     \"\"\"Get estimator tags.\n369: \n370:     :class:`~sklearn.BaseEstimator` provides the estimator tags machinery.\n371:     However, if an estimator does not inherit from this base class, we should\n372:     fall-back to the default tags.\n373: \n374:     For scikit-learn built-in estimators, we should still rely on\n375:     `self.__sklearn_tags__()`. `get_tags(est)` should be used when we\n376:     are not sure where `est` comes from: typically\n377:     `get_tags(self.estimator)` where `self` is a meta-estimator, or in\n378:     the common checks.\n379: \n380:     .. versionadded:: 1.6\n381: \n382:     Parameters\n383:     ----------\n384:     estimator : estimator object\n385:         The estimator from which to get the tag.\n386: \n387:     Returns\n388:     -------\n389:     tags : :class:`~.sklearn.utils.Tags`\n390:         The estimator tags.\n391:     \"\"\"\n392: \n393:     tag_provider = _find_tags_provider(estimator)\n394: \n395:     if tag_provider == \"__sklearn_tags__\":\n396:         # TODO(1.7): turn the warning into an error\n397:         try:\n398:             tags = estimator.__sklearn_tags__()\n399:         except AttributeError as exc:\n400:             if str(exc) == \"'super' object has no attribute '__sklearn_tags__'\":\n401:                 # workaround the regression reported in\n402:                 # https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/30479\n403:                 # `__sklearn_tags__` is implemented by calling\n404:                 # `super().__sklearn_tags__()` but there is no `__sklearn_tags__`\n405:                 # method in the base class.\n406:                 warnings.warn(\n407:                     f\"The following error was raised: {str(exc)}. It seems that \"\n408:                     \"there are no classes that implement `__sklearn_tags__` \"\n409:                     \"in the MRO and/or all classes in the MRO call \"\n410:                     \"`super().__sklearn_tags__()`. Make sure to inherit from \"\n411:                     \"`BaseEstimator` which implements `__sklearn_tags__` (or \"\n412:                     \"alternatively define `__sklearn_tags__` but we don't recommend \"\n413:                     \"this approach). Note that `BaseEstimator` needs to be on the \"\n414:                     \"right side of other Mixins in the inheritance order. The \"\n415:                     \"default are now used instead since retrieving tags failed. \"\n416:                     \"This warning will be replaced by an error in 1.7.\",\n417:                     category=DeprecationWarning,\n418:                 )\n419:                 tags = default_tags(estimator)\n420:             else:\n421:                 raise\n422:     else:\n423:         # Note that most common\n        tests currently run only if this flag is set to ``True``.\n\n    three_d_array : bool, default=False\n        Whether the input can be a 3D array.\n\n    sparse : bool, default=False\n        Whether the input can be a sparse matrix.\n\n    categorical : bool, default=False\n        Whether the input can be categorical.\n\n    string : bool, default=False\n        Whether the input can be an array-like of strings.\n\n    dict : bool, default=False\n        Whether the input can be a dictionary.\n\n    positive_only : bool, default=False\n        Whether the estimator requires positive X.\n\n    allow_nan : bool, default=False\n        Whether the estimator supports data with missing values encoded as `np.nan`.\n\n    pairwise : bool, default=False\n        This boolean attribute indicates whether the data (`X`),\n        :term:`fit` and similar methods consists of pairwise measures\n        over samples rather than a feature representation for each\n        sample.  It is usually `True` where an estimator has a\n        `metric` or `affinity` or `kernel` parameter with value\n        'precomputed'. Its primary purpose is to support a\n        :term:`meta-estimator` or a cross validation procedure that\n        extracts a sub-sample of data intended for a pairwise\n        estimator, where the data needs to be indexed on both axes.\n        Specifically, this tag is used by\n        `sklearn.utils.metaestimators._safe_split` to slice rows and\n        columns.\n\n        Note that if setting this tag to ``True`` means the estimator can take only\n        positive values, the `positive_only` tag must reflect it and also be set to\n        ``True``.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    one_d_array: bool = False\n    two_d_array: bool = True\n    three_d_array: bool = False\n    sparse: bool = False\n    categorical: bool = False\n    string: bool = False\n    dict: bool = False\n    positive_only: bool = False\n    allow_nan: bool = False\n    pairwise: bool = False\n\n\n@dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\nclass TargetTags:\n    \"\"\"Tags for the target data.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    required : bool\n        Whether the estimator requires y to be passed to `fit`,\n        `fit_predict` or `fit_transform` methods. The tag is ``True``\n        for estimators inheriting from `~sklearn.base.RegressorMixin`\n        and `~sklearn.base.ClassifierMixin`.\n\n    one_d_labels : bool, default=False\n        Whether the input is a 1D labels (y).\n\n    two_d_labels : bool, default=False\n        Whether the input is a 2D labels (y).\n\n    positive_only : bool, default=False\n        Whether the estimator requires a positive y (only applicable\n        for regression).\n\n    multi_output : bool, default=False\n        Whether a regressor supports multi-target outputs or a classifier supports\n        multi-class multi-output.\n\n        See :term:`multi-output` in the glossary.\n\n    single_output : bool, default=True\n        Whether the target can be single-output. This can be ``False`` if the\n        estimator supports only multi-output cases.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    required: bool\n    one_d_labels: bool = False\n    two_d_labels: bool = False\n    positive_only: bool = False\n    multi_output: bool = False\n    single_output: bool = True\n\n\n@dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\nclass TransformerTags:\n    \"\"\"Tags for the transformer.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    preserves_dtype : list[str], default=[\"float64\"]\n        Applies only on transformers. It corresponds to the data types\n        which will be preserved such that `X_trans.dtype` is the same\n        as `X.dtype` after calling `transformer.transform(X)`. If this\n        list is empty, then the transformer is not expected to\n        preserve the data type. The first value in the list is\n        considered as the default data type, corresponding to the data\n        type of the output when the input data type is not going to be\n        preserved.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    preserves_dtype: list[str] = field(default_factory=lambda: [\"float64\"])\n\n\n@dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\nclass ClassifierTags:\n    \"\"\"Tags for the classifier.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    poor_score : bool, default=False\n        Whether the estimator fails to provide a \"reasonable\" test-set\n        score, which currently for classification is an accuracy of\n        0.83 on ``make_blobs(n_samples=300, random_state=0)``. The\n        datasets and values are based on current estimators in scikit-learn\n        and might be replaced by something more systematic.\n\n    multi_class : bool, default=True\n        Whether the classifier can handle multi-class\n        classification. Note that all classifiers support binary\n        classification. Therefore this flag indicates whether the\n        classifier is a binary-classifier-only or not.\n\n        See :term:`multi-class` in the glossary.\n\n    multi_label : bool, default=False\n        Whether the classifier supports multi-label output: a data point can\n        be predicted to belong to a variable number of classes.\n\n        See :term:`multi-label` in the glossary.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    poor_score: bool = False\n    multi_class: bool = True\n    multi_label: bool = False\n\n\n@dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\nclass RegressorTags:\n    \"\"\"Tags for the regressor.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    poor_score : bool, default=False\n        Whether the estimator fails to provide a \"reasonable\" test-set\n        score, which currently for regression is an R2 of 0.5 on\n        ``make_regression(n_samples=200, n_features=10,\n        n_informative=1, bias=5.0, noise=20, random_state=42)``. The\n        dataset and values are based on current estimators in scikit-learn\n        and might be replaced by something more systematic.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    poor_score: bool = False\n\n\n@dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\nclass Tags:\n    \"\"\"Tags for the estimator.\n\n    See :ref:`estimator_tags` for more information.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    estimator_type : str or None\n        The type of the estimator. Can be one of:\n        - \"classifier\"\n        - \"regressor\"\n        - \"transformer\"\n        - \"clusterer\"\n        - \"outlier_detector\"\n        - \"density_estimator\"\n\n    target_tags : :class:`TargetTags`\n        The target(y) tags.\n\n    transformer_tags : :class:`TransformerTags` or None\n        The transformer tags.\n\n    classifier_tags : :class:`ClassifierTags` or None\n        The classifier tags.\n\n    regressor_tags : :class:`RegressorTags` or None\n        The regressor tags.\n\n    array_api_support : bool, default=False\n        Whether the estimator supports Array API compatible inputs.\n\n    no_validation : bool, default=False\n        Whether the estimator skips input-validation. This is only meant for\n        stateless and dummy transformers!\n\n    non_deterministic : bool, default=False\n        Whether the estimator is not deterministic given a fixed ``random_state``.\n\n    requires_fit : bool, default=True\n        Whether the estimator requires to be fitted before calling one of\n        `transform`, `predict`, `predict_proba`, or `decision_function`.\n\n    _skip_test : bool, default=False\n        Whether to skip common tests entirely. Don't use this unless\n        you have a *very good* reason.\n\n    input_tags : :class:`InputTags`\n        The input data(X) tags.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    estimator_type: str | None\n    target_tags: TargetTags\n    transformer_tags: TransformerTags | None = None\n    classifier_tags: ClassifierTags | None = None\n    regressor_tags: RegressorTags | None = None\n    array_api_support: bool = False\n    no_validation: bool = False\n    non_deterministic: bool = False\n    requires_fit: bool = True\n    _skip_test: bool = False\n    input_tags: InputTags = field(default_factory=InputTags)\n\n\n# TODO(1.8): Remove this function\ndef default_tags(estimator) -> Tags:\n    \"\"\"Get the default tags for an estimator.\n\n    This ignores any ``__sklearn_tags__`` method that the estimator may have.\n\n    If the estimator is a classifier or a regressor, ``target_tags.required``\n    will be set to ``True``, otherwise it will be set to ``False``.\n\n    ``transformer_tags`` will be set to :class:`~.sklearn.utils. TransformerTags` if the\n    estimator has a ``transform`` or ``fit_transform`` method, otherwise it will be set\n    to ``None``.\n\n    ``classifier_tags`` will be set to :class:`~.sklearn.utils.ClassifierTags` if the\n    estimator is a classifier, otherwise it will be set to ``None``.\n    a classifier, otherwise it will be set to ``None``.\n\n    ``regressor_tags`` will be set to :class:`~.sklearn.utils.RegressorTags` if the\n    estimator is a regressor, otherwise it will be set to ``None``.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    estimator : estimator object\n        The estimator for which to get the default tags.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    tags : Tags\n        The default tags for the estimator.\n    \"\"\"\n    est_is_classifier = getattr(estimator, \"_estimator_type\", None) == \"classifier\"\n    est_is_regressor = getattr(estimator, \"_estimator_type\", None) == \"regressor\"\n    target_required = est_is_classifier or est_is_regressor\n\n    return Tags(\n        estimator_type=getattr(estimator, \"_estimator_type\", None),\n        target_tags=TargetTags(required=target_required),\n        transformer_tags=(\n            TransformerTags()\n            if hasattr(estimator, \"transform\") or hasattr(estimator, \"fit_transform\")\n            else None\n        ),\n        classifier_tags=ClassifierTags() if est_is_classifier else None,\n        regressor_tags=RegressorTags() if est_is_regressor else None,\n    )\n\n\n# TODO(1.7): Remove this function\ndef _find_tags_provider(estimator, warn=True):\n    \"\"\"Find the tags provider for an estimator.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    estimator : estimator object\n        The estimator to find the tags provider for.\n\n    warn : bool, default=True\n        Whether to warn if the tags provider is not found.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    tag_provider : str\n        The tags provider for the estimator. Can be one of:\n        - \"_estimator_tags\": to use the old tags infrastructure\n        - \"__sklearn_tags__\": to use the new tags infrastructure\n    \"\"\"\n    mro_model = type(estimator).mro()\n    tags_mro = OrderedDict()\n    for klass in mro_model:\n        tags_provider = []\n        if \"_more_tags\" in vars(klass):\n            tags_provider.append(\"_more_tags\")\n        if \"_estimator_tags\" in vars(klass):\n            tags_provider.append(\"_estimator_tags\")\n        if \"__sklearn_tags__\" in vars(klass):\n            tags_provider.append(\"__sklearn_tags__\")\n        tags_mro[klass.__name__] = tags_provider\n\n    all_providers = set(chain.from_iterable(tags_mro.values()))\n    if \"__sklearn_tags__\" not in all_providers:\n        # default on the old tags infrastructure\n        return \"_estimator_tags\"\n\n    tag_provider = \"__sklearn_tags__\"\n    for klass in tags_mro:\n        has_get_or_more_tags = any(\n            provider in tags_mro[klass] for provider in (\"_estimator_tags\", \"_more_tags\")\n        )\n        has_sklearn_tags = \"__sklearn_tags__\" in tags_mro[klass]\n\n        if tags_mro[klass] and tag_provider == \"__sklearn_tags__\":  # is it empty\n            if has_get_or_more_tags and not has_sklearn_tags:\n                # Case where a class does not implement __sklearn_tags__ and we fallback\n                # to _estimator_tags. We should therefore warn for implementing\n                # __sklearn_tags__.\n                tag_provider = \"_estimator_tags\"\n                break\n\n    if warn and tag_provider == \"_estimator_tags\":\n        warnings.warn(\n            f\"The {estimator.__class__.__name__} or classes from which it inherits \"\n            \"use `_estimator_tags` and `_more_tags`. Please define the \"\n            \"`__sklearn_tags__` method, or inherit from `sklearn.base.BaseEstimator` \"\n            \"and/or other appropriate mixins such as `sklearn.base.TransformerMixin`, \"\n            \"`sklearn.base.ClassifierMixin`, `sklearn.base.RegressorMixin`, and \"\n            \"`sklearn.base.OutlierMixin`. Note that most common\n        tests currently run only if this flag is set to ``True``.\n\n    three_d_array : bool, default=False\n        Whether the input can be a 3D array.\n\n    sparse : bool, default=False\n        Whether the input can be a sparse matrix.\n\n    categorical : bool, default=False\n        Whether the input can be categorical.\n\n    string : bool, default=False\n        Whether the input can be an array-like of strings.\n\n    dict : bool, default=False\n        Whether the input can be a dictionary.\n\n    positive_only : bool, default=False\n        Whether the estimator requires positive X.\n\n    allow_nan : bool, default=False\n        Whether the estimator supports data with missing values encoded as `np.nan`.\n\n    pairwise : bool, default=False\n        This boolean attribute indicates whether the data (`X`),\n        :term:`fit` and similar methods consists of pairwise measures\n        over samples rather than a feature representation for each\n        sample.  It is usually `True` where an estimator has a\n        `metric` or `affinity` or `kernel` parameter with value\n        'precomputed'. Its primary purpose is to support a\n        :term:`meta-estimator` or a cross validation procedure that\n        extracts a sub-sample of data intended for a pairwise\n        estimator, where the data needs to be indexed on both axes.\n        Specifically, this tag is used by\n        `sklearn.utils.metaestimators._safe_split` to slice rows and\n        columns.\n\n        Note that if setting this tag to ``True`` means the estimator can take only\n        positive values, the `positive_only` tag must reflect it and also be set to\n        ``True``.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    one_d_array: bool = False\n    two_d_array: bool = True\n    three_d_array: bool = False\n    sparse: bool = False\n    categorical: bool = False\n    string: bool = False\n    dict: bool = False\n    positive_only: bool = False\n    allow_nan: bool = False\n    pairwise: bool = False\n\n\n@dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\nclass TargetTags:\n    \"\"\"Tags for the target data.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    required : bool\n        Whether the estimator requires y to be passed to `fit`,\n        `fit_predict` or `fit_transform` methods. It corresponds to the data types\n        which will be preserved such that `X_trans.dtype` is the same\n        as `X.dtype` after calling `transformer.transform(X)`. If this\n        list is empty, then the transformer is not expected to\n        preserve the data type. The first value in the list is\n        considered as the default data type, corresponding to the data\n        type of the output when the input data type is not going to be\n        preserved.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    preserves_dtype: list[str] = field(default_factory=lambda: [\"float64\"])\n\n\n@dataclass(**_dataclass_args())\nclass ClassifierTags:\n    \"\"\"Tags for the classifier.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    poor_score : bool, default=False\n        Whether the estimator fails to provide a \"reasonable\" test-set\n        score, which currently for classification is an accuracy of\n        0.83 on ``make_blobs(n_samples=300, random_state=0)``. The\n        datasets and values are based on current estimators in scikit-learn\n        and might be replaced by something more systematic.\n\n    multi_class : bool, default=True\n        Whether the classifier can handle multi-class\n        classification. Can be one of:\n        - \"_estimator_tags\": to use the old tags infrastructure\n        - \"__sklearn_tags__\": to use the new tags infrastructure\n    \"\"\"\n    mro_model = type(estimator).mro()\n    tags_mro = OrderedDict()\n    for klass in mro_model:\n        tags_provider = []\n        if \"_more_tags\" in vars(klass):\n            tags_provider.append(\"_more_tags\")\n        if \"_estimator_tags\" in vars(klass):\n            tags_provider.append(\"_estimator_tags\")\n        if \"__sklearn_tags__\" in vars(klass):\n            tags_provider.append(\"__sklearn_tags__\")\n        tags_mro[klass.__name__] = tags_provider\n\n    all_providers = set(chain.from_iterable(tags_mro.values()))\n    if \"__sklearn_tags__\" not in all_providers:\n        # default on the old tags infrastructure\n        return \"_estimator_tags\"\n\n    tag_provider = \"__sklearn_tags__\"\n    for klass in tags_mro:\n        has_get_or_more_tags = any(\n            provider in tags_mro[klass] for provider in (\"_estimator_tags\", \"_more_tags\")\n        )\n        has_sklearn_tags = \"__sklearn_tags__\" in tags_mro[klass]\n\n        if tags_mro[klass] and tag_provider == \"__sklearn_tags__\":  # is it empty\n            if has_get_or_more_tags and not has_sklearn_tags:\n                # Case where a class does not implement __sklearn_tags__ and we fallback\n                # to _estimator_tags. From scikit-learn 1.7, not defining \"\n            \"`__sklearn_tags__` will raise an error.\",\n            category=DeprecationWarning,\n        )\n    return tag_provider\n\n\ndef get_tags(estimator) -> Tags:\n    \"\"\"Get estimator tags.\n\n    :class:`~sklearn.BaseEstimator` provides the estimator tags machinery.\n    However, if an estimator does not inherit from this base class, we should\n    fall-back to the default tags.\n\n    For scikit-learn built-in estimators, we should still rely on\n    `self.__sklearn_tags__()`. `get_tags(est)` should be used when we\n    are not sure where `est` comes from: typically\n    `get_tags(self.estimator)` where `self` is a meta-estimator, or in\n    the common checks.\n\n    .. versionadded:: 1.6\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    estimator : estimator object\n        The estimator from which to get the tag.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    tags : :class:`~.sklearn.utils.Tags`\n        The estimator tags.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    tag_provider = _find_tags_provider(estimator)\n\n    if tag_provider == \"__sklearn_tags__\":\n        # TODO(1.7): turn the warning into an error\n        try:\n            tags = estimator.__sklearn_tags__()\n        except AttributeError as exc:\n            if str(exc) == \"'super' object has no attribute '__sklearn_tags__'\":\n                # workaround the regression reported in\n                # https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/30479\n                # `__sklearn_tags__` is implemented by calling\n                # `super().__sklearn_tags__()` but there is no `__sklearn_tags__`\n                # method in the base class.\n                warnings.warn(\n                    f\"The following error was raised: {str(exc)}. It seems that \"\n                    \"there are no classes that implement `__sklearn_tags__` \"\n                    \"in the MRO and/or all classes in the MRO call \"\n                    \"`super().__sklearn_tags__()`. Make sure to inherit from \"\n                    \"`BaseEstimator` which implements `__sklearn_tags__` (or \"\n                    \"alternatively define `__sklearn_tags__` but we don't recommend \"\n                    \"this approach). Note that `BaseEstimator` needs to be on the \"\n                    \"right side of other Mixins in the inheritance order. The \"\n                    \"default are now used instead since retrieving tags failed. \"\n                    \"This warning will be replaced by an error in 1.7.\",\n                    category=DeprecationWarning,\n                )\n                tags = default_tags(estimator)\n            else:\n                raise\n    else:\n        # TODO(1.7): Remove this branch of the code\n        # Let's go through the MRO and patch each class implementing _more_tags\n        sklearn_tags_provider = {}\n        more_tags_provider = {}\n        class_order = []\n        for klass in reversed(type(estimator).mro()):\n            if \"__sklearn_tags__\" in vars(klass):\n                sklearn_tags_provider[klass] = klass.__sklearn_tags__(estimator)  # Find differences between consecutive in the case of __sklearn_tags__\n        # inheritance\n        sklearn_tags_diff = {}\n        items = list(sklearn_tags_provider.items())\n        for current_item, next_item in zip(items[:-1], items[1:]):\n            current_name, current_tags = current_item\n            next_name, next_tags = next_item\n            current_tags = _to_old_tags(current_tags)\n            next_tags = _to_old_tags(next_tags)\n\n            # Compare tags and store differences\n            diff = {}\n            for key in current_tags:\n                if current_tags[key] != next_tags[key]:\n                    diff[key] = next_tags[key]\n\n            sklearn_tags_diff[next_name] = diff\n\n        tags = {}\n        for klass in class_order:\n            if klass in sklearn_tags_diff:\n                tags.update(sklearn_tags_diff[klass])\n            elif klass in more_tags_provider:\n                tags.update(more_tags_provider[klass])\n\n        tags = _to_new_tags(\n            {**_to_old_tags(default_tags(estimator)), **tags}, estimator\n        )\n\n    return tags\n\n\n# TODO(1.7): Remove this function\ndef _safe_tags(estimator, key=None):\n    warnings.warn(\n        \"The `_safe_tags` function is deprecated in 1.6 and will be removed in \"\n        \"1.7. Use the public `get_tags` function instead and make sure to implement \"\n        \"the `__sklearn_tags__` method.\",\n        category=DeprecationWarning,\n    )\n    tags = _to_old_tags(get_tags(estimator))\n\n    if key is not None:\n        if key not in tags:\n            raise ValueError(\n                f\"The key {key} is not defined for the class \"\n                f\"{estimator.__class__.__name__}.\"\n            )\n        return tags[key]\n    return tags\n\n\n# TODO(1.7): Remove this function\ndef _to_new_tags(old_tags, estimator=None):\n    \"\"\"Utility function convert old tags (dictionary) to new tags (dataclass).\"\"\"\n    input_tags = InputTags(\n        one_d_array=\"1darray\" in old_tags[\"X_types\"],\n        two_d_array=\"2darray\" in allow_nan=old_tags[\"allow_nan\"],\n        pairwise=old_tags[\"pairwise\"],\n    )\n    target_tags = TargetTags(\n        required=old_tags[\"requires_y\"],\n        one_d_labels=\"1dlabels\" in old_tags[\"multioutput_only\"],\n        single_output=not old_tags[\"multioutput_only\"],\n    )\n    if estimator is not None and (\n        hasattr(estimator, \"transform\") or hasattr(estimator, \"fit_transform\")\n    ):\n        transformer_tags = TransformerTags(\n            preserves_dtype=old_tags[\"preserves_dtype\"],\n        )\n    else:\n        transformer_tags = None\n It returns a new estimator\\n49:     with the same parameters that has not been fitted on any data.\\n50: \\n51:     .. versionchanged:: 1.3\\n52:         Delegates to `estimator.__sklearn_clone__` if the method exists.\\n53: \\n54:     Parameters\\n55:     ----------\\n56:     estimator : {list, tuple, set} of estimator instance or a single \\\\\\n57:             estimator instance\\n58:         The estimator or group of estimators to be cloned.\\n59:     safe : bool, default=True\\n60:         If safe is False, clone will fall back to a deep copy on objects\\n61:         that are not estimators. Ignored if `estimator.__sklearn_clone__`\\n62:         exists.\\n63: \\n64:     Returns\\n65:     -------\\n66:     estimator : object\\n67:         The deep copy of the input, an estimator if input is an estimator.\\n68: \\n69:     Notes\\n70:     -----\\n71:     If the estimator's `random_state` parameter is an integer (or if the\\n72:     estimator doesn't have a `random_state` parameter), an *exact clone* is\\n73:     returned: the clone and the original estimator will give the exact same\\n74:     results. Otherwise, *statistical clone* is returned: the clone might\\n75:     return different results from the original estimator. More details can be\\n76:     found in :ref:`randomness`.\\n77: \\n78:     Examples\\n79:     --------\\n80:     >>> from sklearn.base import clone\\n81:     >>> from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression\\n82:     >>> X = [[-1, 0], [0, 1], [0, -1], [1, 0]]\\n83:     >>> y = [0, 0, 1, 1]\\n84:     >>> classifier = LogisticRegression().fit(X, y)\\n85:     >>> cloned_classifier = clone(classifier)\\n86:     >>> hasattr(classifier, \\\"classes_\\\")\\n87:     True\\n88:     >>> hasattr(cloned_classifier, \\\"classes_\\\")\\n89:     False\\n90:     >>> classifier is cloned_classifier\\n91:     False\\n92:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n93:     if hasattr(estimator, \\\"__sklearn_clone__\\\") and not inspect.isclass(estimator):\\n94:         return estimator.__sklearn_clone__()\\n95:     return _clone_parametrized(estimator, safe=safe)\\n96: \\n97: \\n98: def _clone_parametrized(estimator, *, safe=True):\\n99:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Default implementation of clone. in estimator.items()}\\n104:     elif estimator_type in (list, tuple, set, frozenset):\\n105:         return estimator_type([clone(e, safe=safe) for e in estimator])\\n106:     elif not   Notes\\n172:     -----\\n173:     All estimators should specify all the parameters that can be set\\n174:     at the class level in their ``__init__`` as explicit keyword\\n175:     arguments (no @classmethod\\n201:     def _get_param_names(cls):\\n202:         \\\"\\\"\\\"Get parameter names for the estimator\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n203:         # fetch the constructor or the original constructor \\n255:     def set_params(self, **params):\\n256:         \\\"\\\"\\\"Set the parameters of this estimator.\\n257: \\n258:         The method works on simple estimators as well as on nested objects\\n259:         (such as :class:`~sklearn.pipeline.Pipeline`). The latter have\\n260:         parameters of the form ``<component>__<parameter>`` so that it's\\n261:         possible to update each component of a nested object.\\n262: \\n263:         Parameters\\n264:         ----------\\n265:         **params : dict\\n266:             Estimator parameters.\\n267: \\n268:         Returns\\n269:         -------\\n270:         self : estimator instance\\n271:             Estimator instance.\\n272:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n273:         if not params:\\n274:             # Simple optimization to gain speed (inspect is slow)\\n275:             return self\\n276:         valid_params = self.get_params(deep=True)\\n277: \\n278:         nested_params = defaultdict(dict)  # grouped by prefix\\n279:         for key, value in params.items():\\n280:             key, delim, sub_key = key.partition(\\\"__\\\")\\n281:             if key not in valid_params:\\n282:                 local_valid_params = self._get_param_names()\\n283:                 raise ValueError(\\n284:                     f\\\"Invalid parameter {key!r} for estimator {self}. \\\"\\n285:                     f\\\"Valid parameters are: {local_valid_params!r}.\\\"\\n286:                 )\\n287: \\n288:             if delim:\\n289:                 nested_params[key][sub_key] = value\\n290:             else:\\n291:                 setattr(self, key, value)\\n292:                 valid_params[key] = value\\n293: \\n294:         for key, sub_params in nested_params.items():\\n295:             valid_params[key].set_params(**sub_params)\\n296: \\n297:         return self\\n298: \\n299:     def __sklearn_clone__(self):\\n300:         return _clone_parametrized(self)\\n301: \\n302:     def __repr__(self, N_CHAR_MAX=700):\\n303:         # N_CHAR_MAX is the (approximate) maximum number of non-blank\\n304:         # characters to render. We pass it as an optional parameter to ease\\n305:         # the tests.\\n306: \\n307:         from .utils._pprint import _EstimatorPrettyPrinter\\n308: \\n309:         N_MAX_ELEMENTS_TO_SHOW = 30  # number of elements to show in sequences\\n310: \\n311:         # use ellipsis for sequences with a lot of elements\\n312:         pp = _EstimatorPrettyPrinter(\\n313:             compact=True,\\n314:             indent=1,\\n315:             indent_at_name=True,\\n316:             n_max_elements_to_show=N_MAX_ELEMENTS_TO_SHOW,\\n317:         )\\n318: \\n319:         repr_ = pp.pformat(self)\\n320: \\n321:         # Use bruteforce ellipsis when there are a lot of non-blank characters\\n322:         n_nonblank = len(\\\"\\\".join(repr_.split()))\\n323:         if n_nonblank > N_CHAR_MAX:\\n324:             lim = N_CHAR_MAX // 2  # apprx number of chars to keep on both ends\\n325:             regex = r\\\"^(\\\\s*\\\\S){%d}\\\" % lim\\n326:             # The regex '^(\\\\s*\\\\S){%d}' % n\\n327:             # matches from the start of the string until the nth non-blank\\n328:             # character:\\n329:             # - ^ matches the start of string\\n330:             # - (pattern){n} matches n repetitions of pattern\\n331:             # - \\\\s*\\\\S matches a non-blank char following zero or more blanks\\n332:             left_lim = re.match(regex, repr_).end()\\n333:             right_lim = re.match(regex, repr_[::-1]).end()\\n334: \\n335:             if \\\"\\\\n\\\" in repr_[left_lim:-right_lim]:\\n336:                 # The left side and right side aren't on the same line.\\n337:                 # To avoid weird cuts, e.g.:\\n338:                 # categoric...ore',\\n339:                 # we need to start the right side with an appropriate newline\\n340:                 # character so that it renders properly as:\\n341:                 # categoric...\\n342:                 # handle_unknown='ignore',\\n343:                 # so we add [^\\\\n]*\\\\n which matches until the next \\\\n\\n344:                 regex += r\\\"[^\\\\n]*\\\\n\\\"\\n345:                 right_lim = re.match(regex, repr_[::-1]).end()\\n346: \\n347:             ellipsis = \\\"...\\\"\\n348:             if left_lim + len(ellipsis) < len(repr_) - right_lim:\\n349:                 # Only add ellipsis if it results in a shorter repr\\n350:                 repr_ = repr_[:left_lim] + \\\"...\\\" + repr_[-right_lim:]\\n351: \\n352:         return repr_\\n353: \\n354:     def __getstate__(self):\\n355:         if getattr(self, \\\"__slots__\\\", None):\\n356:             raise TypeError(\\n357:                 \\\"You cannot use `__slots__` in objects inheriting from \\\"\\n358:                 \\\"`sklearn.base.BaseEstimator`.\\\"\\n359:             )\\n360: \\n361:         try:\\n362:             state = super().__getstate__()\\n363:             if state is None:\\n364:                 # \\\"\\\"\\\"Mixin class for all classifiers in scikit-learn.\\n504: \\n505:     This mixin defines the following functionality:\\n506: \\n507:     - set estimator type to `\\\"classifier\\\"` through the `estimator_type` tag;\\n508:     - `score` method that default to :func:`~sklearn.metrics.accuracy_score`.\\n509:     - enforce that `fit` requires `y` to be passed through the `requires_y` tag,\\n510:       which is done by setting the classifier type tag.\\n511: \\n512:     Read more in the :ref:`User Guide <rolling_your_own_estimator>`.\\n513: \\n514:     Examples\\n515:     --------\\n516:     >>> import numpy as np\\n517:     >>> from sklearn.base import BaseEstimator, ClassifierMixin\\n518:     >>> # Mixin classes should always be on the left-hand side for a correct MRO\\n519:     >>> class MyEstimator(ClassifierMixin, BaseEstimator):\\n520:     ...     def __init__(self, *, param=1):\\n521:     ...         self.param = param\\n522:     ...     def fit(self, X, y=None):\\n523:     ...         self.is_fitted_ = True\\n524:     ...         return self\\n525:     ...     def predict(self, X):\\n526:     ...         return np.full(shape=X.shape[0], fill_value=self.param)\\n527:     >>> estimator = MyEstimator(param=1)\\n528:     >>> X = np.array([[1, 2], [2, 3], [3, 4]])\\n529:     >>> y = np.array([1, 0, 1])\\n530:     >>> estimator.fit(X, \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n548:         Return the mean accuracy on the given test data and labels.\\n549: \\n550:         In multi-label classification, this is the subset accuracy\\n551:         which is a harsh metric since you require for each sample that\\n552:         each label set be correctly predicted.\\n553: \\n554:         Parameters\\n555:         ----------\\n556:         X : array-like of shape (n_samples, n_features)\\n557:             Test samples.\\n558: \\n559:         y : array-like of shape (n_samples,) or (n_samples, n_outputs)\\n560:             True labels for `X`.\\n561: \\n562:         sample_weight : array-like of shape (n_samples,), default=None\\n563:             Sample weights.\\n564: \\n565:         Returns\\n566:         -------\\n567:         score : float\\n568:             Mean accuracy of ``self.predict(X)`` w.r.t. `y`.\\n569:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n570:         from .metrics import accuracy_score\\n571: \\n572:         return accuracy_score(y, self.predict(X), sample_weight=sample_weight)\\n573: \\n574: \\n575: class RegressorMixin:\\n576:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Mixin class for all regression estimators in scikit-learn.\\n577: \\n578:     This mixin defines the following functionality:\\n579: \\n580:     - set estimator type to `\\\"regressor\\\"` through the `estimator_type` tag;\\n581:     - `score` method that default to :func:`~sklearn.metrics.r2_score`.\\n582:     - enforce that `fit` requires `y` to be passed through the `requires_y` tag,\\n583:       which is done by setting the regressor type tag.\\n584: \\n585:     Read more in the :ref:`User Guide <rolling_your_own_estimator>`.\\n586: \\n587:     Examples\\n588:     --------\\n589:     >>> import numpy as np\\n590:     >>> from sklearn.base import BaseEstimator, RegressorMixin\\n591:     >>> # Mixin classes should always be on the left-hand side for a correct MRO\\n592:     >>> class MyEstimator(RegressorMixin, BaseEstimator):\\n593:     ... self\\n598:     ...     def predict(self, X):\\n599:     ...         return np.full(shape=X.shape[0], fill_value=self.param)\\n600:     >>> estimator = MyEstimator(param=0)\\n601:     >>> X = np.array([[1, 2], [2, 3], [3, 4]])\\n602:     >>> y = np.array([-1, 0, 1])\\n603:     >>> estimator.fit(X, y).predict(X)\\n604:     array([0, 0, 0])\\n605:     >>> estimator.score(X, y)\\n606:     0.0\\n607:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n608: \\n609:     # TODO(1.8): Remove this attribute\\n610:     _estimator_type = \\\"regressor\\\"\\n611: \\n612:     def __sklearn_tags__(self):\\n613:         tags = return tags\\n618: \\n619:     def score(self, X, y, sample_weight=None):\\n620:         \\\"\\\"\\\"Return the coefficient of determination of the prediction.\\n621: \\n622:         The coefficient of determination :math:`R^2` is defined as\\n623:         :math:`(1 - \\\\\\\\frac{u}{v})`, where :math:`u` is the residual\\n624:         sum of squares ``((y_true - y_pred)** 2).sum()`` and :math:`v`\\n625:         is the total sum of squares ``((y_true - y_true.mean()) ** 2).sum()``.\\n626:         The best possible score is 1.0 and it can be negative (because the\\n627:         model can be arbitrarily worse). A constant model that always predicts\\n628:         the expected value of `y`, disregarding the input features, would get\\n629:         a :math:`R^2` score of 0.0.\\n630: \\n631:         Parameters\\n632:         ----------\\n633:         X : array-like of shape (n_samples, n_features)\\n634:             Test samples. For some estimators this may be a precomputed\\n635:             kernel matrix or a list of generic objects instead with shape\\n636:             ``(n_samples, n_samples_fitted)``, where ``n_samples_fitted``\\n637:             is the number of samples used in the fitting for the estimator.\\n638: \\n639:         y : array-like of shape (n_samples,) or (n_samples, n_outputs)\\n640:             True values for `X`.\\n641: \\n642:         sample_weight : array-like of shape (n_samples,), default=None\\n643:             Sample weights.\\n644: \\n645:         Returns\\n646:         -------\\n647:         score : float\\n648:             :math:`R^2` of ``self.predict(X)`` w.r.t. `y`.\\n649: \\n650:         Notes\\n651:         -----\\n652:         The :math:`R^2` score used when calling ``score`` on a regressor uses\\n653:         ``multioutput='uniform_average'`` from version 0.23 to keep consistent\\n654:         with default value of :func:`~sklearn.metrics.r2_score`.\\n655:         This influences the ``score`` method of all the multioutput\\n656:         regressors (except for\\n657:         :class:`~sklearn.multioutput.MultiOutputRegressor`).\\n658:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n659: \\n660:         from .metrics import r2_score\\n661: \\n662:         y_pred = self.predict(X)\\n663:         return r2_score(y, y_pred, sample_weight=sample_weight)\\n664: \\n665: \\n666: class ClusterMixin:\\n667:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Mixin class for all cluster estimators in scikit-learn.\\n668: \\n669:     - **kwargs)\\n720:         return self.labels_\\n721: \\n722: \\n723: class BiclusterMixin:\\n724:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Mixin class for all bicluster estimators in scikit-learn.\\n725: \\n726:     This mixin defines the following functionality:\\n727: \\n728:     - `biclusters_` property that returns the row and column indicators;\\n729:     - `get_indices` method that returns the row and column indices of a bicluster;\\n730:     - `get_shape` method that returns the shape of a bicluster;\\n731:     - `get_submatrix` method that returns the submatrix corresponding to a bicluster.\\n732: \\n733:     Examples\\n734:     --------\\n735:     >>> import numpy as np\\n736:     >>> from sklearn.base import BaseEstimator, BiclusterMixin\\n737:     >>> class DummyBiClustering(BiclusterMixin, BaseEstimator):\\n738:     ...     def fit(self, X, y=None):\\n739:     ...         self.rows_ = np.ones(shape=(1, X.shape[0]), dtype=bool)\\n740:     ...         self.columns_ = np.ones(shape=(1, get_indices(self, i):\\n760:         \\\"\\\"\\\"Row and column indices of the `i`'th bicluster.\\n761: \\n762:         Only works if ``rows_`` and ``columns_`` attributes exist.\\n763: \\n764:         Parameters\\n765:         ----------\\n766:         i : int\\n767:             The index of the cluster.\\n768: \\n769:         Returns\\n770:         -------\\n771:         row_ind : ndarray, dtype=np.intp\\n772:             Indices of rows in the dataset that belong to the bicluster.\\n773:         col_ind : ndarray, dtype=np.intp\\n774:             Indices of columns in the dataset that belong to the bicluster.\\n775:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n776:         rows = self.rows_[i]\\n777:         columns = self.columns_[i]\\n778:         return np.nonzero(rows)[0], np.nonzero(columns)[0]\\n779: \\n780:     def get_shape(self, i):\\n781:         \\\"\\\"\\\"Shape of the `i`'th bicluster.\\n782: \\n783:         Parameters\\n784:         ----------\\n785:         i : int\\n786:             The index of the cluster.\\n787: \\n788:         Returns\\n789:         -------\\n790:         n_rows : int\\n791:             Number of rows in the bicluster.\\n792: \\n793: ----------\\n804:         i : int\\n805:             The index of the cluster.\\n806:         data : array-like of shape (n_samples, n_features)\\n807:             The data.\\n808: \\n809: -----\\n816:         Works with sparse matrices. See the :ref:`developer_api_set_output` for details.\\n836: \\n837:     :class:`OneToOneFeatureMixin` and\\n838:     :class:`ClassNamePrefixFeaturesOutMixin` are helpful mixins for\\n839:     defining :term:`get_feature_names_out`.\\n840: \\n841:     Examples\\n842:     --------\\n843:     >>> import numpy as np\\n844:     >>> from sklearn.base import BaseEstimator, TransformerMixin\\n845:     >>> class MyTransformer(TransformerMixin, BaseEstimator):\\n846:     ...     def __init__(self, *, param=1):\\n847:     ...         self.param = param\\n848:     ...     def fit(self, X, y=None):\\n849:     ...         return self\\n850:     ...     def transform(self, X):\\n851:     ...         return np.full(shape=len(X), fill_value=self.param)\\n852:     >>> transformer = MyTransformer()\\n853:     >>> X = [[1, 2], [2, 3], [3, 4]]\\n854:     >>> transformer.fit_transform(X)\\n855:     array([1, 1, 1])\\n856:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n857: \\n858:     def __sklearn_tags__(self):\\n859:         tags = super().__sklearn_tags__()\\n860:         tags.transformer_tags = TransformerTags()\\n861:         return tags\\n862: \\n863:     def fit_transform(self, X, y=None, **fit_params):\\n864:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n865:         Fit to data, then transform it.\\n866: \\n867:         Fits transformer to `X` and `y` with optional parameters `fit_params`\\n868:         and returns a transformed version of `X`.\\n869: \\n870:         Parameters\\n871:         ----------\\n872:         X : array-like of shape (n_samples, n_features)\\n873:             Input samples.\\n874: \\n875:         y :  array-like of shape (n_samples,) or (n_samples, n_outputs), \\\\\\n876:                 default=None\\n877:             Target values (None for unsupervised transformations).\\n878: \\n879:         **fit_params : dict\\n880:             Additional fit parameters.\\n881: \\n882:         Returns\\n883:         -------\\n884:         X_new : ndarray array of shape (n_samples, n_features_new)\\n885:             Transformed array.\\n886:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n887:         # non-optimized default implementation; override when a better\\n888:         # method is possible for a given clustering algorithm\\n889: \\n890:         # we do not route parameters here, since consumers don't route. But\\n891:         # since it's possible for a `transform` method to also consume\\n892:         # metadata, we check if that's the case, and we raise a warning telling\\n893:         # users that they should implement a custom `fit_transform` method\\n894:         # to forward metadata to `transform` as well.\\n895:         #\\n896:         # For that, we calculate routing and check if anything would be routed\\n897:         # to `transform` if we were to route them.\\n898:         if _routing_enabled():\\n899:             transform_params = self.get_metadata_routing().consumes(\\n900:                 method=\\\"transform\\\", params=fit_params.keys()\\n901:             )\\n902:             if transform_params:\\n903:                 warnings.warn(\\n904:                     (\\n905:                         f\\\"This object ({self.__class__.__name__}) has a `transform`\\\"\\n906:                         \\\" method which consumes metadata, but `fit_transform` does not\\\"\\n907:                         \\\" forward metadata to `transform`. Please implement a custom\\\"\\n908:                         \\\" `fit_transform` method to forward metadata to `transform` as\\\"\\n909:                         \\\" well. If `feature_names_in_` is not defined,\\n953:               then the following input feature names are generated:\\n954:               `[\\\"x0\\\", \\\"x1\\\", ..., \\\"x(n_features_in_ - 1)\\\"]`.\\n955:             - If `input_features` is an array-like, then `input_features` must\\n956:               match `feature_names_in_` if `feature_names_in_` is defined.\\n957: \\n958:         Returns\\n959:         -------\\n960:         feature_names_out : ndarray of str objects\\n961:             Same as input features.\\n962:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n963:         # Note that passing attributes=\\\"n_features_in_\\\" forces check_is_fitted\\n964:         # to check if the attribute is present. Otherwise it will pass on\\n965:         # stateless estimators (requires_fit=False)\\n966:         check_is_fitted(self, attributes=\\\"n_features_in_\\\")\\n967:         return _check_feature_names_in(self, input_features)\\n968: \\n969: \\n970: class ClassNamePrefixFeaturesOutMixin:\\n971:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Mixin class for transformers that generate their own names by prefixing.\\n972: \\n973:     This mixin is useful when the transformer needs to generate its own feature\\n974:     names out, such as :class:`~sklearn.decomposition.PCA`. For example, if\\n975:     :class:`~sklearn.decomposition.PCA` outputs 3 features, then the generated feature\\n976:     names out are: `[\\\"pca0\\\", \\\"pca1\\\", \\\"pca2\\\"]`.\\n977: \\n978:     This mixin assumes that a `_n_features_out` attribute is defined when the\\n979:     transformer is fitted. `_n_features_out` is the number of output features\\n980:     that the transformer will return in `transform` of `fit_transform`.\\n981: \\n982:     Examples\\n983:     --------\\n984:     >>> import numpy as np\\n985:     >>> from sklearn.base import ClassNamePrefixFeaturesOutMixin, BaseEstimator\\n986:     >>> class MyEstimator(ClassNamePrefixFeaturesOutMixin, BaseEstimator):\\n987:     ...     def fit(self, X, y=None):\\n988:     ...         self._n_features_out = X.shape[1]\\n989:     ...         return self\\n990:     >>> X = np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4]])\\n991:     >>> MyEstimator().fit(X).get_feature_names_out()\\n992:     array(['myestimator0', 'myestimator1'], dtype=object)\\n993:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n994: \\n995:     def get_feature_names_out(self, input_features=None):\\n996:         \\\"\\\"\\\"Get output feature names for transformation.\\n997: \\n998:         The feature names out will prefixed by the lowercased class name. It's also useful to avoid validating\\n1358:         parameters that we pass internally to inner functions that are guaranteed to\\n1359:         be valid by the test suite.\\n1360: \\n1361:         It should be set to True for most estimators, except for those that receive\\n1362:         non-validated objects as parameters, such as meta-estimators that are given\\n1363:         estimator objects.\\n1364: \\n1365:     Returns\\n1366:     -------\\n1367:     decorated_fit : method\\n1368:         The decorated fit method.\\n1369:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n1370: \\n1371:     def decorator(fit_method):\\n1372:         @functools.wraps(fit_method)\\n1373:         def wrapper(estimator, *args, **kwargs):\\n1374:             global_skip_validation = get_config()[\\\"skip_parameter_validation\\\"]\\n1375: \\n1376:             # we don't want to validate again for each call to partial_fit\\n1377:             partial_fit_and_fitted = (\\n1378:                 fit_method.__name__ == \\\"partial_fit\\\" and _is_fitted(estimator)\\n1379:             )\\n1380: \\n1381:             if not global_skip_validation and not partial_fit_and_fitted:\\n1382:                 estimator._validate_params()\\n1383: \\n1384:             with config_context(\\n1385:                 skip_parameter_validation=(\\n1386:                     prefer_skip_nested_validation or global_skip_validation\\n1387:                 )\\n1388:             ):\\n1389:                 return fit_method(estimator, *args, **kwargs)\\n1390: \\n1391:         return wrapper\\n1392: \\n1393:     return decorator\\n1394: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/scikit-learn--1.6.1@ec6ee443c3c32c29d026be5f2e09d6c3de139ce0/-/blob/sklearn/base.py\"}"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01AY1hRBSvgM5AiBPBh2vc1q","session_id":"6a92c968-14c6-4523-90d0-c07e95bd6bba","uuid":"1f16dcbc-aa10-4e98-9962-4f8280548862"}
